preamble: Final-obstruent devoicing is a phonetic process where voiced sibilants, such as the alveolar [z], lose their voicing at the end of a word or syllable. This shift is a characteristic feature of many languages, notably in the Germanic and Slavic families.

examples.note: Standard final-devoicing of sibilants in German.
Dutch singular form shows final devoicing.
Final-obstruent devoicing of sibilants in Polish.
Historical merger of voiced [z] into voiceless [s] in early modern Spanish.

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
